title: Four-shaft monk's belt
subtitle: Threading and treadling a traditional two-colour block weave on a four-shaft loom
excerpt: Monk's belt is a traditional block weave found in Scandinavian and early American textiles. This entry covers the threading, tie-up, and treadling for a classic two-colour monk's belt sample on a four-shaft loom, producing a bold geometric pattern on a plain-weave ground.
sourceNotes: Monk's belt is documented in Mary E. Black, The Key to Weaving (1945, in-copyright, substance only referenced), and in Harriet Tidball, The Handweaver's Instruction Manual (1949, in-copyright, substance only referenced). The structure also appears in Mary Atwater, The Shuttle-Craft Book of American Hand-Weaving (1928, in-copyright, substance only referenced).

glossaryTerms.definition: The pattern by which each warp thread is assigned to a specific shaft. In monk's belt, shafts 1 and 2 form the plain-weave ground; shafts 3 and 4 carry the pattern weft floats.
The connection pattern between treadles and shafts on a floor loom. In monk's belt, the tie-up determines which treadle raises the ground shafts and which raises the pattern shafts for each block.

The threads stretched lengthways on the loom, through which the weft is passed. In monk's belt, the warp is typically a plain ground colour; the pattern is created by a contrasting pattern weft that floats over groups of warp threads.

content.text: Monk's belt is a four-shaft block weave with a simple setup. The
follows a simple four-thread repeat, the
uses four treadles. Pattern bands form where the weft floats over groups of
threads to form each block. Use a bright pattern weft against a neutral ground warp for the classic Scandinavian look.

items.name: Ground warp yarn, natural cotton or linen (8/2 weight)
Pattern weft yarn, contrasting colour (8/2 or 10/2 weight)
Boat shuttle for ground weft, stick shuttle for pattern weft

content.text: Thread on a straight draw: shaft 1, shaft 2, shaft 3, shaft 4, repeated across the full warp width. This gives equal numbers of threads on each shaft. Sley at 20 ends per 10 cm (epi 10) in an 8-dent reed (2 threads per dent) for a balanced cloth at 8/2 sett.

Tie treadle 1 to shaft 1, treadle 2 to shaft 2, treadle 3 to shafts 1 and 3, treadle 4 to shafts 2 and 4. To weave a plain-weave band, alternate treadles 1 and 2. To weave a pattern band (two-block design), alternate treadles 3 and 4 with the pattern weft while continuing to weave plain weave with the ground weft between each pattern pick.

Cut the sample off the loom and secure the warp ends with hemstitching or a simple knotted fringe. Wet-finish by hand-washing in warm soapy water, gently rolling in a towel, and blocking flat. Cotton and linen monk's belt will full slightly and become more stable after the first wash.

items.problem: The pattern weft floats are too long and sag in the finished cloth
items.cause: The pattern block is too wide relative to the weft yarn weight
items.fix: Reduce the width of each pattern block by weaving fewer pattern picks per block, or use a heavier pattern weft that sits more firmly over the floats.

items.problem: The plain-weave ground shows uneven spacing between pattern bands
items.cause: Inconsistent beat between pattern and ground bands
items.fix: Beat each pick at the same firmness using the same wrist motion. Use a temple to keep the selvedges parallel.
